title: Ambiguity in POS Tagging
concept: The same word can have different POS tags depending on context (e.g., 'fly' can be a noun or a verb), which models must resolve.

exercises.prompt: Before we start, what does 'POS' stand for in natural language processing?
exercises.explain: POS stands for 'Part of Speech', which includes grammatical categories like nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs.

cards.title: The Double-Agent Words
cards.body:
If someone says the word 'light', what comes to your mind? Is it a lamp shining in the dark (a noun)? Or is it a box that doesn't weigh very much (an adjective)? 

Without a full sentence, even you can't be sure! This is the puzzle of POS ambiguity that AI models face every day.

cards.title: What is POS Ambiguity?
cards.body: Ambiguity in Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging happens when a single word can have multiple meanings and grammatical roles. Because computers don't naturally understand human context, they have to analyze the neighboring words to figure out which tag fits best.

cards.title: The Chameleon Word
cards.body: Imagine the word 'run' is like a chameleon. If you put it on a track, it turns into an action (a verb): 'I run every morning.' If you put it in a baseball game, it turns into a point (a noun): 'We scored a run!' Just like a chameleon, words change their 'color' based on their surroundings.

cards.title: One Word, Two Roles
cards.body:
Take the word 'fly':
• 'Look at that fly!' -> Here, 'fly' is a Noun (an insect).
• 'Birds fly high.' -> Here, 'fly' is a Verb (the action of flying).

An NLP model looks at words like 'that' (which often points to nouns) or 'birds' (which perform actions) to make the right guess!

cards.title: Myth: Dictionaries Solve Everything
cards.body: Many people think that a dictionary is all an AI needs to tag words. In reality, a dictionary only lists the possibilities. The AI must look at the context—the words before and after—to solve the mystery of which tag is correct.

cards.title: Same Word, Different Tags
cards.body: See how the same word gets completely different tags depending on the context of the sentence!

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Can you open the can of soup?', how does the word 'can' behave?

options.text: The first 'can' is a verb, and the second 'can' is a noun.
The first 'can' is a noun, and the second 'can' is a verb.

exercises.explain: In 'Can you open the can of soup?', the first 'can' expresses ability (a helping verb), while the second 'can' is a metal container (a noun).

exercises.prompt: True or False: A single English word can have different POS tags depending on the sentence it is used in.
exercises.explain: Many words in English, such as 'run', 'light', 'cook', and 'bank', can be nouns, verbs, or adjectives depending on where they are placed in a sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept to complete the explanation.
exercises.sentence: To resolve POS ambiguity, an AI model must look at the surrounding [blank] of a word.
exercises.explain: An AI model cannot tag a word correctly just by looking at the word itself; it must analyze the surrounding context (the neighboring words) to resolve ambiguity.

exercises.prompt: An AI model is tagging sentences. Which of these tags is INCORRECT?

options.text: 'My watch is gold.' -> 'watch' is tagged as a NOUN.
'I watch TV every night.' -> 'watch' is tagged as a NOUN.
'I watch TV every night.' -> 'watch' is tagged as a VERB.

exercises.explain: The second sentence represents incorrect tagging. 'Watch' is an action (verb) in 'I watch TV', but the model incorrectly labeled it as a thing (noun).

exercises.prompt:
Look at these two sentences: 
1. 'Where should I park the car?'
2. 'Let\'s go play in the park.'

Identify the correct POS tags for the word 'park' in Sentence 1 and Sentence 2.
exercises.explain: The word 'park' functions as a verb (an action) in the first sentence because it describes what you do with the car, and as a noun (a place) in the second sentence because it is a location. POS taggers must look at the surrounding context to decide the correct tag!
